title: SentencePiece: Raw Text Processing
concept: SentencePiece tokenizes directly from raw text bytes, treating spaces as characters ('_'), avoiding language-specific pre-tokenizers.

exercises.prompt: What does SentencePiece process directly to create tokens?
exercises.explain: SentencePiece is designed to process raw text directly without needing a separate pre-tokenization step that splits text by spaces.

cards.title: The Space Dilemma
cards.body: Most AI tokenizers get confused by languages like Chinese or Japanese because they don't use spaces to separate words. How do we teach an AI to read text when there are no spaces to guide it? Enter SentencePiece!

cards.title: What is SentencePiece?
cards.body: SentencePiece is a tokenizer that works directly on raw text. Instead of relying on rules to split words by spaces first, it treats spaces just like any other character (using a special symbol like '_'). This makes it work incredibly well for all languages, even those without spaces!

cards.title: The Laser Cookie Cutter
cards.body: Think of traditional tokenizers like cookie cutters that only work on dough already sliced into words. SentencePiece is like a laser cutter: it doesn't care if there are spaces, punctuation, or even different alphabets. It looks at the raw sheet of text and cuts exactly where needed, even if it has to slice right through a word.

cards.title: SentencePiece in Action
cards.body:
In English, the sentence "I love coding" is usually split by spaces first. 

SentencePiece turns it into: 
`_I`, `_love`, `_cod`, `ing`

Because the space is kept as `_`, we can easily glue the pieces back together to get the exact original text without losing any spacing details!

cards.title: The Space Myth
cards.body: Many people think you must split text into words using spaces before an AI can understand it. That's a myth! SentencePiece proves that treating spaces as just another character actually makes tokenization cleaner and universal across all languages.

cards.title: Comparing Tokenizers
cards.body: Traditional tokenizers must split by spaces first, which fails on some languages. SentencePiece treats spaces as characters, making it work for everything.

right.text: Treats spaces as '_'. Works on raw text bytes for any language.

left.text: Splits by spaces first. Needs different rules for different languages.

exercises.prompt: What is a major advantage of SentencePiece over older tokenization methods?

options.text: It does not require pre-splitting text by spaces.
It converts all text to uppercase.

exercises.explain: SentencePiece processes raw text directly, meaning it does not need a language-specific ruleset to split words by spaces beforehand.

exercises.prompt: True or False: SentencePiece can be used on languages that do not use spaces between words.
exercises.explain: SentencePiece is language-independent because it treats text as a raw stream of characters/bytes, making it perfect for languages without spaces like Chinese.

exercises.prompt: SentencePiece keeps track of spaces by replacing them with which special character?
exercises.sentence: SentencePiece replaces spaces with the [blank] character.
exercises.explain: SentencePiece replaces normal spaces with a special underscore character (_) so it can treat them like any other letter.

exercises.prompt: Which tokenized output was created by SentencePiece?
exercises.explain: The text containing '_' is processed by SentencePiece, which treats spaces as characters. The other texts are either standard word splits or character splits.

exercises.prompt: You are building a translation model that needs to support both English and Japanese. Why is SentencePiece a great choice for this task?

options.text: It treats spaces as regular characters ('_') and works directly on raw text bytes.
It requires a pre-tokenizer to split text by spaces first.
It only works on English text.
It deletes all spaces before analyzing the text.

exercises.explain: SentencePiece processes raw bytes and replaces spaces with a special character (like '_') before tokenizing. This means it doesn't need to split text by spaces first, allowing it to work perfectly on languages like Japanese or Chinese that don't use spaces between words.
